svg.text: Neighborly — How data flows to each page
Read left to right. Each page is fed from one place. The goal: every flow runs automatically. Colours = where it comes from; tags = whether it's automated yet.
You forward it each morning; it's read in automatically.
Sales, average check, labor hours & cost, sales-per-
labor-hour, labor %, comps, product mix, dayparts —
by location, for day / week / period / quarter / year.
Product mix (item by item). Labor detail:
overtime, by job, hours before open / after
Events typed into the calendar page.
Today they save on one device only — not shared.
Need a shared database so events sync across
devices and feed the calendar subscription (.ics).
Fed by the daily email — restaurant/catering, day→year. Forecast uses 6-week average until a budget is added.
Totals from email (SPLH, hours, labor %) · overtime, by-job, before/after from Toast
Fed by Toast (item-level). Calculated once here; the Marketing page links to it — no double work.
Manual today — goal is to automate
Not built — needs a database / new feed
Automation checklist — for the tech team
The job: make each page's flow run by itself. One source feeds both portals where it overlaps — build it once.
Daily Sales email → Sales & labor data: already automatic (depends on the email being forwarded each day).
KPI Tracker → Performance Marketing: set up the automatic sync.
Meta export → Social: automate the Facebook/Instagram pull.
Ovation → Guest Intelligence: automate the feedback upload.
Toast → Product Mix + Labor detail: automate the POS export (feeds both the Marketing and Ops mix pages, plus overtime / by-job / before-after).
Marketing Calendar → build a shared database: so events sync across devices instead of saving on one, and feed the .ics subscription.
1) How does each source connect — does it offer an automatic feed (API), or is it an export/upload we schedule?
2) Where should the shared data + calendar database live (which service), and who owns the daily refresh if the email isn't forwarded?
Same data feeds Marketing and Ops — one source of truth, shown two ways.
